title: Algorithms (Java)
description: Build passable interview fluency in Java through practical fundamentals: problem solving, strings, arithmetic/binary conversion, and arrays.

reviewer.summary: For your Java prep, the goal is to memorize the standard code patterns and library choices that solve beginner interview problems fast. Think in terms of code templates, not vague theory.
interviewFocus: Do you know which Java library or class to reach for immediately?
Can you write the standard template from memory with minimal hesitation?
Can you solve simple problems like leap year, reverse string, binary conversion, and array scans in clean Java?
Can you explain why you chose that tool instead of improvising badly?

sections.title: Must Know In Time For Interviews
points: Memorize clean Java templates for input parsing, if-else decision problems, StringBuilder usage, arithmetic/base conversion loops, and array traversal.
Know the standard library tools that commonly appear in simple coding rounds: BufferedReader, StringTokenizer, Scanner, StringBuilder, Arrays, ArrayList, HashMap, and Math.
Practice small real tasks like even or odd, leap year, prime number, palindrome number, factorial, Fibonacci, sum of digits, reverse digits, GCD or LCM, and finding factors or divisors.

sections.title: Suggested Crash-Course Sequence
points: Start with input parsing until reading ints, strings, arrays, and test cases feels automatic.
Then drill core number problems like parity, leap year, prime checks, palindrome numbers, factorial, Fibonacci, digit sums, reverse digits, GCD or LCM, and divisors.
Then memorize string templates with StringBuilder and charAt.
After that, rehearse arithmetic and binary conversion loops, then finish with array traversal and Arrays helper methods.

sections.title: What Usually Fails People
points: Forgetting how to read input quickly and wasting time on boilerplate under pressure.
Forgetting the exact Java library or method signature they actually need.
Writing code from first principles every time instead of using a memorized template.
Making index and condition mistakes on easy problems because the base pattern was not fully internalized.

chapters.title: Input Parsing
chapters.focus: Read ints, strings, arrays, and test cases without fumbling Java I/O.

chapters.title: Numbers
chapters.focus: Master the standard number problems that show up constantly in beginner interviews.

chapters.title: String Manipulation
chapters.focus: Traverse, compare, rebuild, and inspect strings safely.

chapters.title: Arithmetic & Binary Conversion
chapters.focus: Work through number logic, divisibility, and base conversion step by step.

chapters.title: Arrays
chapters.focus: Scan, update, and summarize arrays without index mistakes.

questions.prompt: For competitive-style Java input, which base setup is the strongest default to memorize first?
questions.codeSnippet:
// goal: fast enough for many interview / CP input problems
// choose the base setup you should know cold

choices.label: BufferedReader + StringTokenizer + StringBuilder
JOptionPane dialogs for each number
Recursive input parsing methods only
Random access files for every coding problem

questions.explanation: If you want one Java I/O toolkit to memorize for interviews and competitive programming, BufferedReader for input, StringTokenizer for token splitting, and StringBuilder for output is the strongest default.

questions.prompt: You need to read two integers from one line like `5 9`. Which code pattern should you memorize?
questions.codeSnippet:
BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(new InputStreamReader(System.in));
// input line: 5 9

choices.label: StringTokenizer st = new StringTokenizer(br.readLine()); int a = Integer.parseInt(st.nextToken()); int b = Integer.parseInt(st.nextToken());
int a = br.readLine(); int b = br.readLine();
StringTokenizer st = new StringTokenizer(); int a = st; int b = st;
int a = Integer.parseInt(br); int b = Integer.parseInt(br);

questions.explanation: This is the bread-and-butter pattern: read the line, tokenize it, then parse each token into the target type.

questions.prompt: You are given `n`, then a second line with `n` space-separated integers. What pattern is correct?

choices.label: Read n, create int[] arr = new int[n], tokenize the next line, fill arr in a loop
Read n and assume Java fills the array automatically from stdin
Only parse the first token because arrays can resize later
Sort before reading the values

questions.explanation: A lot of problems use the exact pattern: read size first, then tokenize the next line and fill the array sequentially.

questions.prompt: Many problems begin with a test-case count `t`. Which loop structure should feel automatic?
questions.codeSnippet:
// first line contains t
// solve one case per iteration

choices.label: int t = Integer.parseInt(br.readLine()); while (t-- > 0) { /* solve one case */ }
int t = Integer.parseInt(br.readLine()); if (t > 0) { /* solve all cases once */ }
int t = Integer.parseInt(br.readLine()); for (;;) { /* stop when lucky */ }
No loop is needed because t is only metadata

questions.explanation: When a problem gives a test-case count, your body template should already be ready. The exact loop form can vary, but `while (t-- > 0)` is a standard one worth memorizing.

questions.prompt: Why is `StringBuilder` important in Java I/O-heavy problems?
questions.codeSnippet: // imagine printing one answer per test case

choices.label: It helps accumulate output efficiently before printing once
It is useful for building strings in loops without repeated costly concatenation
It automatically parses integers from input
It is often cleaner than many System.out.println calls inside tight loops

questions.explanation: StringBuilder is mainly about output construction efficiency and cleaner repeated string assembly. It is not an input parser.

questions.prompt: Which first-principles checklist is strongest when reading a new Java input format?
questions.codeSnippet: // before coding the solution, decode the input shape

choices.label: How many lines or tokens belong to one test case?
What type is each token: int, long, string, char?
Do I need an array, matrix, or just scalar variables?
Can I ignore the input format and improvise later?

questions.explanation: Good Java problem solving starts by decoding the input contract. Once you know the tokens, their types, and the per-case shape, the parsing code becomes mechanical.
questions.studyNote:
Input decoding checklist

Ask first:
1. Is there a test-case count?
2. What does one case contain?
3. Which tokens are ints / longs / strings?
4. Do I need scalar variables, an array, or a matrix?
5. Which line should be tokenized next?

questions.prompt: What is the right way to complete this leap-year style problem in Java?

choices.label: A direct if / else-if / else chain that encodes the stated rules
A nested loop because every decision problem needs iteration
A recursive method because it looks more advanced
A HashMap because rule checks should be data-driven by default

questions.explanation: For rule-based problems like leap year, the right move is usually a direct conditional chain that mirrors the stated logic instead of forcing in loops or more advanced structures.

questions.prompt: You need to reverse a string and also solve many similar rebuilding tasks. Which Java tools/patterns should be in your base kit?

choices.label: Know when to use charAt and length safely
Track indices carefully when comparing from both ends
Convert every string problem into recursion first
Use StringBuilder when repeatedly constructing output

questions.explanation: String questions are usually about safe indexing, clean comparisons, and not doing expensive string concatenation carelessly in loops.

questions.prompt: Which code pattern should you memorize for manual decimal-to-binary conversion in Java?

choices.label: Repeatedly divide by 2, append remainder, then reverse
Sort the digits first, then join them
Use nested loops because base conversion needs two dimensions
Convert the number to a char array before doing arithmetic

questions.explanation: For manual binary conversion, the standard interview-safe loop is divide by 2, record remainders, then reverse the collected digits.

questions.prompt: For beginner array problems in Java, which tools and habits are worth memorizing first?

choices.label: Use a for loop or enhanced for loop to scan once
Know Arrays.sort for ordering-based solutions
Convert every array into recursion before scanning
Track running state like min, max, sum, or count

questions.explanation: Most beginner array problems are solved with one clean scan, optional sorting via Arrays.sort, and disciplined running-state updates.
